content.text: Web research agents autonomously search the open internet, extract and evaluate content from web pages, and synthesise findings into structured reports with citations. They combine search API calls, web scraping, content extraction, and LLM-based synthesis to answer research questions using current web content — providing information that extends beyond LLM training cutoffs and covers recent events, market developments, and emerging technical topics.

attrs.alt: Web Research Agent Architecture: query → search API + web scraper + content extractor → relevance ranker → synthesis LLM → cited report

content.text: Web research agents are autonomous systems that combine search engine APIs, web scraping, content extraction, and LLM-based reasoning to answer research questions using live internet content. They differ from static RAG systems (which query a pre-indexed corpus) by actively searching the open web at query time, retrieving and parsing pages, and synthesising findings from real-time sources. This makes them uniquely suited to questions about recent events, current market conditions, and rapidly evolving technical topics.

The core architecture involves: (1) a search query generator that formulates effective search engine queries from a natural language objective; (2) search API integration (Google Custom Search, Bing Search API, Tavily, Serper) that returns result URLs and snippets; (3) a web scraper or content extractor (Playwright for JavaScript-heavy pages, Firecrawl for general-purpose crawling) that retrieves full-page text; (4) a relevance ranker that scores and deduplicates retrieved passages; and (5) a synthesis LLM that writes a structured report with inline citations.

Web research agents face challenges not present in closed-corpus RAG: dynamic content (JavaScript-rendered pages), anti-scraping mechanisms (CAPTCHAs, rate limits, bot detection), temporal freshness management, source credibility assessment, and prompt injection via malicious web content. Production agents must handle these robustly to deliver reliable results.

LLM training data has a knowledge cutoff — any development after the training date is invisible to parametric knowledge. Web research agents bridge this gap by grounding LLM synthesis in current web content. This is critical for: competitive analysis of companies that launched after the model's cutoff, technical research on tools and frameworks that release updates monthly, financial research on recent earnings and market conditions, and news analysis on ongoing events.

Web research also provides traceability that parametric LLM answers cannot. A web research agent cites specific URLs and passage excerpts, allowing downstream verification. This is increasingly important as AI-generated content becomes prevalent and users need to distinguish AI synthesis from primary sources.
Current information: accesses web content from today, not the training cutoff date.
Source traceability: every claim links to a specific URL and passage excerpt.
Breadth: searches across thousands of potential sources in seconds.
Query adaptability: generates multiple search queries with different angles to cover the topic from different perspectives.
No pre-indexing required: retrieves from the live web without a preprocessing pipeline or maintained vector index.
Multi-source synthesis: integrates findings from news, documentation, forums, academic sites, and industry reports.
Search API costs: high-volume web search (Bing/Google) is more expensive than local vector search.
Anti-scraping challenges: many sites block automated access; some require JavaScript execution (Playwright).
Source credibility: web content ranges from authoritative to completely fabricated; agents need credibility heuristics.
Latency: scraping multiple pages adds 3-10 seconds of I/O overhead per query.
Prompt injection: malicious pages can embed instructions targeting the synthesis LLM.
Content churn: pages change or disappear; citations may become invalid quickly.
Paywalled and account-gated content: a significant portion of valuable web content is behind login walls.

Query generation is the first step. A planning LLM takes the research objective and generates 3-5 diverse search queries covering different angles, keywords, and entity variations. Diversity is important: multiple queries targeting the same high-ranking source waste API calls, while diverse queries cast a wider net across the web graph.

Search APIs return a list of result URLs with titles and snippets. The agent fetches the full text of the top N results using an HTTP client or headless browser. Content extraction strips navigation, advertisements, footers, and boilerplate, leaving only the main article text. Tools like Trafilatura and Readability.js achieve 85-95% precision on news articles and documentation pages.

Passages are scored for relevance against the research query using embedding similarity or an LLM reranker, then deduplicated to remove near-identical passages that appear across multiple pages. The top-ranked passages are assembled into a context window and passed to the synthesis LLM with a citation prompt. The synthesis prompt instructs the model to use inline numbered citations and to indicate when multiple sources agree or contradict.

The diagram above shows the web research agent architecture. Queries fan out simultaneously to the Search API (returning URLs), Web Scraper (fetching full page text), and Content Extractor (cleaning boilerplate). Results converge in the Relevance Ranker which scores and deduplicates passages. The Synthesis LLM receives ranked passages and writes a cited report.
When to Use Web Research Agents
Current events and news: information from the past days or weeks.
Competitive analysis: recent product launches, pricing changes, job postings.
Technical documentation research: latest API changes, framework releases.
Market research: recent industry reports, analyst commentary.
Regulatory and legal updates: recent rulings, new regulations, policy changes.

Not appropriate for: research over private or internal documents (use RAG), research requiring structured academic databases (use literature review agents), or tasks with strict latency requirements under 500 ms.

import asyncio
import httpx
import anthropic
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class WebResult:
    url: str
    title: str
    content: str
    score: float = 0.0

async def search_tavily(query: str, api_key: str, max_results: int = 5) -> list[WebResult]:
    """Search using Tavily Research API."""
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        resp = await client.post(
            "https://api.tavily.com/search",
            json={
                "api_key": api_key,
                "query": query,
                "search_depth": "advanced",
                "max_results": max_results,
                "include_raw_content": True,
            },
            timeout=30,
        )
    results = resp.json().get("results", [])
    return [
        WebResult(
            url=r["url"],
            title=r["title"],
            content=(r.get("raw_content") or r.get("content", ""))[:2000],
            score=r.get("score", 0.5),
        )
        for r in results
    ]

async def multi_query_research(
    objective: str,
    tavily_key: str,
    n_queries: int = 4,
) -> str:
    """Generate multiple queries, run in parallel, synthesise."""
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    # Generate diverse search queries
    query_response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"""
Generate {n_queries} diverse search engine queries for this research objective.
Return a JSON list of query strings.

Objective: {objective}
"""}]
    )
    text = query_response.content[0].text
    start, end = text.find("["), text.rfind("]") + 1
    import json
    queries = json.loads(text[start:end]) if start >= 0 else [objective]

    # Run searches in parallel
    tasks = [search_tavily(q, tavily_key) for q in queries[:n_queries]]
    all_results_nested = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

    # Flatten and deduplicate by URL
    seen_urls: set[str] = set()
    results: list[WebResult] = []
    for batch in all_results_nested:
        for r in batch:
            if r.url not in seen_urls:
                seen_urls.add(r.url)
                results.append(r)

    # Sort by score
    results.sort(key=lambda x: x.score, reverse=True)

    # Build context for synthesis
    context = "

".join([
        f"[{i+1}] {r.url}
{r.content[:1500]}"
        for i, r in enumerate(results[:12])
    ])

    # Synthesise
    synthesis = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=3000,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"""
Write a comprehensive research report on: {objective}

Web sources (cite by number):
{context}

Requirements:
- Use inline citations [1], [2], etc.
- Highlight where sources agree and disagree
- Note recency of information
- Conclude with key findings
"""}]
    )
    return synthesis.content[0].text

# Run
import os
result = asyncio.run(multi_query_research(
    "Latest developments in open-source LLM fine-tuning tooling 2024",
    tavily_key=os.environ["TAVILY_API_KEY"],
))
print(result)

from playwright.async_api import async_playwright
import asyncio

async def scrape_js_page(url: str) -> str:
    """Scrape a JavaScript-rendered page with Playwright."""
    async with async_playwright() as p:
        browser = await p.chromium.launch(headless=True)
        page = await browser.new_page(
            user_agent="Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36"
        )
        try:
            await page.goto(url, wait_until="networkidle", timeout=15000)
            await page.wait_for_timeout(2000)  # wait for dynamic content
            # Extract main content
            content = await page.evaluate("""
                () => {
                    const article = document.querySelector('article, main, [role="main"]');
                    return (article || document.body).innerText;
                }
            """)
            return content[:5000]
        finally:
            await browser.close()

# Usage
content = asyncio.run(scrape_js_page("https://techcrunch.com/2024/01/01/example"))
print(content[:500])

A hedge fund uses a web research agent to track daily developments in the technology sector. The agent runs at 6 AM each weekday, searching for news about 50 portfolio companies plus 20 competitors. For each company, it generates 3 queries (earnings updates, product announcements, executive changes), runs 150 searches in parallel, extracts content from retrieved pages, and synthesises a per-company brief with a sentiment score and key developments flagged.

The briefs are delivered to investment analysts by 7 AM. The agent replaces 2-3 hours of manual news monitoring per analyst per day. Edge cases requiring judgment (ambiguous news interpretation, conflicting reports) are flagged with a low-confidence marker and escalated to the analyst. Total API cost per morning run is approximately $2.50 for 150 Tavily searches and 70 Claude API calls.
Sending raw HTML to the LLM: HTML boilerplate, navigation, and ads inflate token count and degrade synthesis quality; always extract clean text first.
Sequential scraping: fetching pages one at a time is slow; use asyncio to parallelise HTTP requests.
No content freshness check: results from cached or archival URLs may be months or years old; check publication dates.
Ignoring robots.txt: scraping sites that disallow bots violates ToS and can get IPs banned; use compliant scraping services.
Treating all search results as authoritative: the first result is SEO-optimised, not necessarily the most credible.
Use a dedicated research search API (Tavily, Exa) rather than general search APIs — they are optimised for content extraction quality.
Parallelise searches with asyncio.gather to run 5-10 queries concurrently and reduce latency from minutes to seconds.
Apply content extraction (Trafilatura, Readability) before passing page text to the LLM to remove boilerplate.
Score source credibility by domain authority, publication date, and HTTPS status.
Deduplicate passages by embedding similarity (threshold ~0.92) before synthesis to prevent bias from heavily syndicated content.
Implement a content safety filter on scraped content before passing to the synthesis LLM.

Web content is an untrusted input channel. Malicious pages can embed instructions targeting the synthesis LLM (prompt injection), serve different content to scrapers vs. browsers (cloaking), or return misleading metadata. Defend against prompt injection by passing scraped content through a sanitisation step that removes HTML comments, invisible text, and unusually long instruction-like text blocks before sending to the LLM.
Validate all URLs before scraping: block private IP ranges, localhost, internal network addresses.
Set strict timeouts (10-15 s) and size limits (max 100 KB per page) to prevent resource exhaustion.
Rotate user agents and use residential proxy services if scraping at scale to avoid IP bans.
Log all scraped URLs and content checksums for reproducibility and audit.
Apply content safety classification on extracted text before synthesis.
Web research agents provide real-time information access beyond LLM training cutoffs by combining search APIs, web scraping, and LLM synthesis.
Parallelising search queries with asyncio reduces latency from minutes to 5-15 seconds for standard research tasks.
Content extraction (removing boilerplate) before LLM synthesis is critical for quality — sending raw HTML is wasteful and degrades results.
Dedicated research search APIs (Tavily, Exa) produce better extraction quality than general-purpose SERP APIs for synthesis tasks.
Prompt injection via malicious web pages is a real production risk; sanitise scraped content before forwarding to the reasoning LLM.
Deduplication by embedding similarity prevents bias from heavily syndicated content that appears across dozens of sites.
Source credibility scoring (domain authority, publication date, HTTPS) is essential for research tasks where accuracy matters.
